questions.question: Explain the purpose of a function prototype in C. Why is it necessary to declare a function before calling it?
keyPoints: Function prototype declares function's name, return type, and parameter list to the compiler
Enables type checking between arguments passed and parameters expected
Without prototype, compiler assumes int return type which can cause undefined behavior
Allows functions to call other functions defined later in the program (forward references)

questions.question: Differentiate between function declaration and function definition in C with examples.
keyPoints: Declaration tells the compiler function signature without body - e.g., int add(int, int);
Definition includes the actual function body/implementation
One function can have one definition but can be declared multiple times
Declaration introduces the function, definition provides the actual code

questions.question: What is the difference between empty parentheses () and void parameter list (void) in function prototypes?
keyPoints: Empty parentheses () means unspecified parameters - compiler does no checking
(void) explicitly indicates the function takes NO arguments
In C, these are different: foo() vs foo(void)
Using (void) is considered better practice for functions taking no arguments

questions.question: Write a function prototype for a function that: (i) takes two float numbers and returns their product as float, (ii) takes a character and returns its ASCII value as integer.
keyPoints: (i) float multiply(float a, float b); or float multiply(float, float);
(ii) int getASCII(char c); or int getASCII(char);
Parameter names can be omitted in prototypes
Return type must match what the function will return

questions.question: What are the consequences of not providing function prototypes in a C program?
keyPoints: Compiler assumes return type as int by default
No compile-time type checking of arguments
Implicit type conversion may occur leading to unexpected results
May cause undefined behavior if return type is not int
Modern compilers warn about missing prototypes

questions.question: Explain the term 'forward declaration' with respect to function prototypes.
keyPoints: Forward declaration is declaring a function before its definition
Enables calling functions in any order in the source code
Essential for mutually recursive functions
Simply writing the function prototype serves as forward declaration

questions.question: What does the following function prototype indicate? Explain each component: void display(char *message, int count);
keyPoints: void - function returns no value
display - function name
char *message - first parameter is a pointer to character (string), named message
int count - second parameter is an integer named count
Semicolon indicates this is a declaration, not definition

questions.question: Discuss the syntax and components of a function prototype in C. Also explain why parameter names are optional in prototypes with appropriate examples.
keyPoints: General syntax: return_type function_name(parameter_type1 param1, parameter_type2 param2);
Components: return type, function name, parameter list with types
Parameter names can be omitted: int add(int, int); is valid
When to include names: helps document code, matches definition order
When to omit: quick declarations, pointer to functions
Examples showing both valid approaches
Semicolon is mandatory at the end

questions.question: Write a complete C program with proper function prototypes to calculate the area and perimeter of a rectangle. Include functions for input, calculation, and display. Explain the role of function prototypes in this program.
keyPoints: Include appropriate function prototypes at the beginning
Prototype for input: void getDimensions(float *length, float *width);
Prototype for area: float calculateArea(float length, float width);
Prototype for perimeter: float calculatePerimeter(float length, float width);
Prototype for display: void displayResults(float area, float perimeter);
Prototypes enable main to call functions defined after it
Prototypes ensure type safety across function calls
Complete program with all definitions after main

questions.question: Explain the various scenarios where function prototypes become essential in C programming. Include examples of mutually recursive functions and functions using pointers.
keyPoints: When functions call each other defined later (mutual recursion)
When using function pointers
When separating interface from implementation (header files)
For library functions (declared in header files)
To enable type checking of arguments and return values
Example with mutual recursion: even()-odd() functions
Example with pointers: void swap(int *a, int *b); demonstrating pass-by-reference
Prototypes prevent subtle bugs through compile-time checking

questions.question: Compare and contrast: (i) int func(); vs int func(void); (ii) Function declaration vs variable declaration. Provide practical implications of each.
keyPoints: (i) func() means unspecified parameters in C, func(void) means no parameters
In C++ they are the same, but in C they differ
Using func(void) is explicit and safer practice
(ii) Function declaration ends with semicolon, has parentheses
Variable declaration: type variable_name; no parentheses
Function declaration provides signature, variable declares memory allocation
Both provide information to compiler about identifiers
